context: Tests whether the agent correctly uses Loki 3.x structured metadata patterns — keeping high-cardinality identifiers out of stream selectors, positioning structured metadata filters before parsers for bloom filter acceleration, and using auto-detected labels like detected_level.

checklist.name: trace_id not in stream selector
checklist.description: The trace investigation query does NOT place trace_id inside the stream selector braces {trace_id="..."}

checklist.name: trace_id as post-stream filter
checklist.description: The trace investigation query filters trace_id as a label filter AFTER the stream selector (e.g. {app="order-service"} | trace_id="a1b2c3d4...")

checklist.name: Structured metadata before parser
checklist.description: In the trace investigation query, the trace_id or detected_level filter appears BEFORE the json parser (for bloom filter acceleration)

checklist.name: detected_level used
checklist.description: At least one query uses detected_level (the Loki 3.x automatic label) rather than parsing level from JSON to detect error events

checklist.name: user_id not in stream selector
checklist.description: Neither query places user_id inside the stream selector braces

checklist.name: sum by namespace grouping
checklist.description: The per-namespace metric query uses sum by (namespace) or equivalent grouping on the namespace label

checklist.name: High-cardinality warning note
checklist.description: The output document contains a note explaining that placing high-cardinality IDs (trace_id, user_id) in the stream selector causes index bloat or degraded performance

checklist.name: Loki 3.x capabilities identified
checklist.description: The explanation mentions at least one specific Loki 3.x feature by name (structured metadata, bloom filters, detected_level, approx_topk, or pattern match operators)
